section.title: The Great Commission
section.sectionRationale: The Gospel ends on a mountain in Galilee. The risen Jesus, given all authority in heaven and on earth, sends His worshiping disciples to make disciples of all nations, baptizing and teaching, with the promise of His own presence to the end of the age.
section.thesis: The risen and enthroned Christ, holding all authority, commissions His church to make disciples of every nation by baptizing and teaching them, and He guarantees the mission with the promise of His abiding presence until the age is complete.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the eleven meeting the risen Jesus on the mountain in worship and doubt; and the commission grounded in His authority and sealed by His presence.

verses.text: Meanwhile, the eleven disciples went to Galilee, to the mountain Jesus had designated.
When they saw Him, they worshiped Him, but some doubted.
Then Jesus came to them and said, “All {{exousia|authority}} in heaven and on earth has been given to Me.
Therefore go and {{matheteuo|make disciples}} of all {{ethne|nations}}, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,
and teaching them to obey all that I have commanded you. And surely I am with you always, even to the end of the age.”

exousia.gloss: Authority, the right and power to rule. The risen Jesus declares that all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to Him, the dominion of the Son of Man in Daniel 7 now His; the mission rests on His universal, sovereign lordship.

matheteuo.gloss: To make a disciple, to bring someone into committed, learning allegiance to Jesus. It is the central command of the commission: not merely to win converts but to make lifelong followers, marked by baptism and formed by obedient teaching.

ethne.gloss: Nations, peoples, the Gentiles. What was hinted throughout Matthew, the Magi, the centurion, the Canaanite woman, now becomes the explicit scope of the mission: all nations, every people group on earth, are to be discipled.

basic.context: The risen Jesus meets the eleven on a mountain in Galilee, as He had promised. They worship Him, though some still waver. He announces that all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to Him, and on that basis He commissions them: go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them into the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, and teaching them to obey everything He commanded. He seals the charge with a promise: He will be with them always, to the very end of the age.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the climax and key of Matthew. Everything the Gospel has shown, Jesus' authority, His teaching, His reach toward the nations, converges here. The main command is make disciples, with going, baptizing, and teaching as its means. The Trinitarian name and the promise of Christ's presence frame the mission as the ongoing work of the whole church until He returns.
basic.originalLanguages: The risen Jesus claims all exousia, authority, in heaven and on earth. On that basis He commands His people to matheteuo, make disciples, of all the ethne, the nations, every people on earth.
basic.typology: Jesus receives the universal dominion of the Son of Man in Daniel 7. The blessing of all nations promised to Abraham reaches its fulfillment as the gospel goes to every people. The mountain recalls the mountains of revelation throughout Matthew.
basic.christ: Jesus is the risen, enthroned Lord with all authority, the one baptized into whose name people belong to God, the teacher whose commands are to be obeyed, and the ever-present Immanuel, God with us to the end of the age.
basic.god: God the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit is named as the one God into whose name disciples are baptized. God's ancient purpose to bless all nations is now unleashed, and He commits Himself, in Christ, to be present with His people as they carry it out.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man's universal dominion. Genesis 12 promises blessing to all nations through Abraham. Matthew 1 opened with Immanuel, God with us, now fulfilled in the closing promise. Acts 1 sends the church as witnesses to the ends of the earth.
basic.oneStory: The Gospel that began with Immanuel, God with us, ends with the risen Christ promising, I am with you always. All authority is His, the nations are His field, and His presence is His people's power. The story of the kingdom, from Abraham's promised blessing to the nations to the enthroned Son of Man, now becomes the church's mission, and it runs on to the end of the age, when the King who commissions will come again.

context.literary: These verses are the deliberate climax of Matthew, gathering its great themes: Jesus' authority, His fulfillment of the Old Testament, His teaching to be obeyed, and the movement of salvation toward the nations. The mountain setting recalls the Sermon on the Mount and the Transfiguration. The closing promise of presence forms an inclusio with Matthew 1:23, Immanuel, God with us, framing the whole Gospel.
context.historical: Galilee, called Galilee of the Gentiles, is a fitting launch point for a mission to the nations. Baptism marked initiation and allegiance; to baptize into a name was to transfer ownership and identity. Rabbis made disciples, but a mission to disciple all peoples was without parallel. The eleven, a broken and reduced band, are entrusted with a task of global scope, sustained only by Christ's authority and presence.
context.canonical: The commission fulfills the Abrahamic promise that all nations would be blessed, Genesis 12:3, and enacts the Son of Man's dominion over all peoples, Daniel 7:14. The Trinitarian baptismal name coheres with the Father, Son, and Spirit revealed at Jesus' own baptism, Matthew 3. It launches the mission that unfolds in Acts and stands behind the worship of every nation in Revelation 7:9.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground the church's mission in the universal authority of the risen Christ, to define that mission as making disciples of all nations through baptism and teaching, and to assure the church of Christ's abiding presence until the end of the age.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is directly and enduringly prescriptive: the command to make disciples of all nations is given to the church for the whole age. The doubt of some describes honest human frailty even amid worship, and the promise of presence assures every generation that undertakes the task.

debates.question: What is the central command of the Great Commission?

views.label: Make disciples, with going, baptizing, and teaching as its means
views.case: In the Greek, make disciples is the single main imperative, and the surrounding words are participles describing how it is done, so the heart of the mission is forming committed followers of Jesus, not merely traveling or gaining decisions.

views.label: A whole fourfold mandate of going, discipling, baptizing, teaching
views.case: The participles carry real imperatival force alongside the main verb, so the commission genuinely commands the church to go, to baptize, and to teach, not only to make disciples in the abstract; each element is essential to obedience.

debates.bearing: Both agree the goal is disciples of all nations and that going, baptizing, and teaching are integral to it; the grammar centers the aim on disciple-making while the participles make going, baptizing, and teaching indispensable means.

study.originalLanguages: The risen Jesus opens with the ground of everything: all exousia, all authority, in heaven and on earth has been given to Him, the universal dominion of the Son of Man now His by right of resurrection. The therefore that follows makes the mission flow from His lordship. Its central command is matheteuo, to make disciples, committed followers who learn and obey, with going, baptizing, and teaching as the means. And its scope is all the ethne, the nations, every people group on earth, so that the hints of Gentile inclusion scattered through Matthew now become the church's global commission. The King who holds all authority sends His people to every nation, and He does not send them alone.

typology.type: The Son of Man's universal dominion
typology.fulfillment: All authority given to the risen Christ, fulfilling Daniel 7:13-14.

typology.type: Blessing to all nations through Abraham
typology.fulfillment: The gospel discipling all peoples, fulfilling Genesis 12:3 and 22:18.
The risen Christ's promise, I am with you always, fulfilling Matthew 1:23 and closing the Gospel.

study.christ: Jesus is the risen and enthroned Lord to whom all authority in heaven and on earth has been given, the Son of Man of Daniel's vision now reigning. On the strength of that authority He sends His church to make disciples of every nation, and He places Himself within the one divine name, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, into which disciples are baptized. He is the teacher whose commands the nations are to obey, and He is Immanuel to the end, promising, surely I am with you always. The Gospel that began by naming Him God with us closes with His own pledge of unbroken presence, so that the mission is carried not by the strength of a broken band but by the living Christ who goes with them.
study.god: The one God, revealed as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, is named as the God into whose single name disciples are baptized, so that the Trinity stands at the foundation of the church's mission and worship. God's ancient purpose to bless all the nations through Abraham's seed is now unleashed to the ends of the earth, no longer confined to one people. And God commits Himself, in the risen Christ, to be present with His people through the whole task, to the very end of the age, so that the mission is sustained not by human resources but by the promised presence of God Himself.

crossReferences.note: The Son of Man given authority over all peoples and nations, fulfilled in the risen Christ's claim to all authority.
In Abraham all the families of the earth will be blessed, realized in the mission to all nations.
Immanuel, God with us, forming an inclusio with the closing promise of Christ's presence.
You will be My witnesses to the ends of the earth, the commission unfolding in the church's mission.

study.oneStory: The Gospel that opened with Immanuel, God with us, closes with the risen Christ promising, surely I am with you always, to the end of the age. All authority in heaven and on earth is His, the nations are His field, and His presence is His people's power. The whole story, from Abraham's promised blessing to the nations, through the enthroned Son of Man, to the cross and empty tomb, now becomes the church's mission to disciple every people. And it runs on toward the end of the age, when the King who commissions and accompanies His people will return in glory to gather them home.

quiz.q: On what basis does Jesus give the Great Commission?

options.text: On His own all-encompassing authority in heaven and on earth as the risen Lord.
options.note: Verse 18 grounds the therefore of the commission in Christ's universal authority.

options.text: On the disciples' readiness and strong faith.
options.note: Some even doubted; the commission rests on Christ's authority, not the disciples' strength.

options.text: On the size and influence of the early church.
options.note: The eleven were a small, broken band; the basis is Christ's authority, not their numbers.

options.text: On the permission granted by the authorities.
options.note: The authority is Christ's own, given by God, not granted by any human power.

quiz.q: What is the central command of the Great Commission?

options.text: To make disciples of all nations, through going, baptizing, and teaching.
options.note: Make disciples is the main command, with the other actions as its means.

options.text: To travel widely and preach to as many as possible.
options.note: Going is part of it, but the goal is making disciples, not travel or preaching alone.

options.text: To baptize as many people as can be found.
options.note: Baptizing is a means; the aim is committed disciples, formed also by teaching.

options.text: To wait in Jerusalem for the nations to come.
options.note: The command is to go to all nations, not to wait for them.

quiz.q: Into whose name are disciples to be baptized?

options.text: The one name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.
options.note: Verse 19 gives the Trinitarian baptismal name, one name for the three persons.
Baptism is into the triune name here; the single name embraces Father, Son, and Spirit together.

options.text: The names of the apostles who baptize them.
options.note: Baptism is into God's name, not the apostles'.
Jesus specifies the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

quiz.q: What promise seals the Great Commission?

options.text: Jesus' abiding presence: surely I am with you always, to the end of the age.
options.note: Verse 20 closes with Christ's promise of continual presence.

options.text: That the mission will be easy and free of opposition.
options.note: He promises His presence, not the absence of hardship, which this misstates.

options.text: That the disciples will see quick and total success.
options.note: The promise is His presence through the whole age, not guaranteed instant results.

options.text: That the task will be finished within their lifetime.
options.note: He promises to be with them to the end of the age, implying a long mission.

questions: Some worshiped and some doubted, yet Jesus commissioned them all. How does it encourage you that Christ uses imperfect, sometimes doubting disciples for His mission?
The command is to make disciples, not merely to gain decisions. Whom is God calling you to help grow into a lifelong follower of Jesus?
The mission reaches all nations. How does the global scope of Christ's purpose stretch your vision beyond your own circle?
Jesus grounds the mission in His authority and seals it with His presence. Where do you need to trust that He goes with you into a hard obedience?
The Gospel ends with I am with you always. How would living each day conscious of the present, risen Christ change the way you follow Him?

translationNotes.note: But some doubted may mean some among the eleven wavered, or some hesitated before recognizing Him; Matthew records honest human frailty even in the moment of worship.

Go is literally a participle, having gone; make disciples is the main imperative, with going, baptizing, and teaching describing how disciples are made. The name is singular for the three persons, a witness to the Trinity.
The end of the age looks to the consummation Jesus described in the Olivet Discourse; the promise of His presence spans the whole mission until He returns, echoing Immanuel in Matthew 1:23.
